summary: Gruber responds to Tim Bray's argument that Apple should open-source its apps by acknowledging Bray's point that it wouldn't hurt upgrade sales, but arguing the practical complications (forking, Software Update collisions, inter-app integration breakage) make it unworkable. He then reframes the debate entirely: Mac OS X is already deeply hackable through scripting, plug-ins, and Cocoa's dynamic runtime — open source isn't the right mechanism for the tweaks power users want. The real problem is that Apple's AppleScript support is inconsistent and many apps lack proper scripting dictionaries or documented plug-in APIs. Gruber concludes that Apple should invest in better scriptability and extensibility rather than opening source code, because scripts and plug-ins are easier to share, don't interfere with system updates, and solve the actual user need.
key_points: Open-sourcing Apple's apps would create practical nightmares: Software Update collisions, broken inter-app integration, and pressure to replace rather than extend official apps.

Mac OS X is already highly hackable through AppleScript, plug-in APIs, Cocoa's dynamic Objective-C runtime, and Unix scripting — the same extensibility model that makes Firefox and Emacs successful despite being open source.

Even open source flagships like Firefox and Emacs are primarily extended through scripting and plug-ins, not by users compiling custom binaries — proving that extensibility APIs matter more than source access.
Apple's real failing is inconsistent AppleScript support across its own apps, with some apps like Preview lacking even basic scripting dictionaries.
The argument that 'most users don't write scripts' misses the point — one person writes kGTD and thousands of OmniOutliner users benefit.
money_quotes: Mac OS X is a very hackable platform; it just isn't hackable by means of the raw source code to the software.
I don't want the source code to their software; I just want to be able to script the hell out of it.
The benefit of good scripting support isn't derived only by those users who write scripts, but also by those who use them.
Firefox would be a lot more hackable if its source code were closed but the extension interface remained than it would be if the source were kept open but the extension interface were removed.
'Try my script' is a lot more appealing than 'try my custom binary of iCal'.
It only took one Ethan Schoonover to write kGTD, but there are now thousands of OmniOutliner Pro users using it.
Software license agreements are full of bullshit restrictions that aren't enforceable and are contrary to established copyright law.

For Apple and Mac OS X, the sweet spot is providing simple, clear, consistent default features and behaviors for typical users, but providing as much extensibility as possible for hackers to play with.
key_insight: The real solution to power users' frustrations with Apple software isn't open source — it's consistent, well-documented scripting and plug-in APIs that let one person's small hack benefit thousands of users without the chaos of forking.
